::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```{=html}
<span id="arch2-release-metadata" hidden="hidden">{{< meta arch2-version >}}</span>
<img class="front-loop-diagram" src="images/arch2-loop-diagram.svg"
     alt="Architecture 2.0 loop diagram connecting architect intent, one or more agentic participants, system evidence, and loop decision" />
```
:::

# Preface {.unnumbered}

Computer architecture has a new question. For decades the field asked what
machines should be built for new kinds of computation. Capable AI systems now
pose the reverse question of what those systems can do for the practice of
architecture itself. The opportunity is larger than producing more candidates.
AI can help translate workload or product intent into an architecture question,
construct and revise alternatives, produce executable artifacts, call design
tools, choose experiments, interpret results, and prepare a recommendation.
Each of those activities can improve architecture work. Each can also hide a bad
assumption, optimize the wrong abstraction, or make a fluent explanation look
more certain than the experiment supports.

> **Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
> architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate, explore,
> implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions.**

This definition keeps computer architecture at the center. The unit of work is
a bounded architecture study, not an isolated model invocation. The
architectural claim is the unit of review. A design loop is an execution and
analysis mechanism that connects represented state, allowed actions, tool
feedback, evidence, and a decision. Supporting records can make the work
inspectable, but they are not its intellectual destination. The destination is
better architectural understanding, a better artifact, or a better-supported
decision.

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
![](images/arch2-loop-diagram.pdf){width="100%" fig-alt="Architecture 2.0 loop diagram connecting architect intent, one or more agentic participants, system evidence, and a loop decision that returns to the architect"}
:::

Machine learning has long appeared inside architecture mechanisms and bounded
optimization workflows. The present shift is broader. AI can now participate
across more of the process that produces and evaluates a claim about a design.
This book asks what engineering discipline that
expanded role requires. A credible study must connect intent to concrete
alternatives, alternatives to real tools, measurements to architectural
mechanisms, and evidence to a recommendation whose limits remain visible.

## What This Book Teaches {.unnumbered}

The book is designed backward from one test. Given a new, bounded computer
architecture problem within the reader's domain competence, the reader should be
able to decide whether and where AI assistance is useful, produce checkable study
artifacts, and defend an evidence-bounded recommendation that another architect
can inspect and challenge. The six verbs in the definition are recurring
capabilities, not a six-stage pipeline.

- **Formulate:** Translate design intent into a bounded study with an explicit
  baseline, design space, objectives, constraints, claims, and non-goals.
- **Explore:** Choose suitable AI or conventional methods, compare meaningful
  alternatives, and preserve why serious candidates failed or were rejected.
- **Implement:** Turn selected alternatives into checkable artifacts connected
  to the simulators, compilers, RTL, EDA, runtimes, or measurements needed to test
  the claim.
- **Evaluate:** Assess the architecture claim and the contribution of AI
  assistance using justified fidelity, representative workloads, matched
  baselines, correctness checks, uncertainty bounds, and counterevidence.
- **Explain:** Form and test an architectural account of why the result occurred
  through mechanisms such as locality, data movement, contention, parallelism,
  critical paths, or software interfaces.
- **Defend:** Make a bounded recommendation that exposes tradeoffs, evidence
  limits, decision ownership, and the conditions that would reverse it.

Implementation here means enough executable structure to test the study's claim,
not necessarily tapeout-ready hardware. Defense means reasoned engineering
judgment, not certification or a claim of finality.

The book tests these capabilities through two recurring cases. The lighthouse
design request carries the aspiration. It is a compact request for a low-power
RISC-V subsystem for mobile extended reality (XR), phrased as if a future AI
design system should be able to answer it. The AlphaChip war story supplies a
grounded stress test, a prominent and contested AI-assisted chip-placement result
whose public record exposed limits on what outside researchers could replay,
compare, or adjudicate. One shows the reach. The other shows the unresolved
methodological debt. Together they keep the argument attached to both ambition
and practice.

Architecture 2.0 remains computer architecture while borrowing selectively from
other disciplines. Five lineages contribute distinct practices:

- **Experimental science:** Contributes falsifiable claims and counterevidence.
- **Software systems:** Contribute observability, testing, versioning, and
  rollback.
- **EDA:** Contributes executable constraints, tool stages, violations, and
  signoff practice.
- **Systems safety:** Contributes whole-system reasoning, independent challenge,
  and visible responsibility.
- **Benchmarking:** Contributes shared workloads, reporting rules, and
  provenance.

These lineages supply useful methods, not the identity of the field. Later
chapters examine what transfers, what does not, and why architecture requires
its own discipline.

That discipline changes what must be visible. Workload traces, design artifacts,
and tool outputs matter, but so do problem formulations, alternatives, interface
assumptions, experiment choices, mechanism-level explanations, failed attempts,
and rejected candidates. A final design without that trajectory can hide why the
conclusion deserves belief. A complete log without an architecture question or
explanation is not yet an architecture contribution.

What follows is an engineering framework, not a catalog of current agents. A
catalog would age with every model release. The intended contribution is a way to
conduct AI-assisted computer architecture studies as methods become more
capable. A compact study record, a one-page card defined in
@sec-architecture-20-ontology and given as a review template in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card, recurs as an index into that work, but it
never substitutes for architectural quality, novelty, insight, or usefulness.

This boundary is deliberate. The book does not propose replacing computer
architects, EDA, verification, compiler and toolchain engineering, or systems
operations with a general AI layer. It does not claim that a model owns an
architecture decision after emitting a plausible artifact. Those practices
supply the expertise, interfaces, constraints, tests, and measurements that make
architecture work executable and contestable. AI can participate throughout the
study. Accountable people or organizational roles remain responsible for setting
the question, judging the explanation, deciding what the evidence supports, and
limiting how far a recommendation may travel.


## Book Structure {.unnumbered}

The ten chapters follow a high-low-high arc. @sec-moonshot through
@sec-architecture-20-ontology frame the ambition, diagnose why existing
architecture practice strains, and establish the architectural claim, bounded
study, and design loop. @sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust descend into the representations, tool
interfaces, methods, and evidence practices that make AI assistance executable
and checkable. @sec-running-the-loop through @sec-what-architect-owns integrate
those pieces in a constructed study, compare how the discipline changes across
the computing stack, and return to architectural judgment, responsibility, and
the shared infrastructure the field needs.

The book distributes cross-cutting concerns across that arc wherever they change
architecture work. These concerns include proprietary interfaces, adversarial
evidence, feedback cost, field telemetry, organizational incentives, legal and
intellectual-property boundaries, and the education of new architects.

## Who This Book Is For {.unnumbered}

This is a compact synthesis for readers who already know computer architecture
and want a framework for AI-assisted practice. It assumes enough fluency to
follow performance, energy, area, and software-interface tradeoffs and to read
an architecture paper, simulator, workload, or design report. It does not assume
a machine-learning or reinforcement-learning background. The AI ideas needed for
the argument are introduced in an architectural context.

A graduate student should learn to conduct a bounded study rather than merely
acquire a vocabulary. A reviewer should be able to test the claim, mechanism,
comparison, and evidence rather than judge only a generated artifact. An author
should be able to explain where AI contributed and defend the recommendation
without overstating it. A practitioner should be able to decide which work to
delegate and where architectural judgment is still required. The common test is
transfer to a problem other than the lighthouse.

## How to Read This Book {.unnumbered}

Each chapter is framed by a guiding question, its crux, and a short list of what
you will be able to do after reading it. The lighthouse request travels through the book as a small mental
simulator, with each return activating a different architecture obligation.
AlphaChip returns at key checkpoints as a grounded stress test. When the text or
a figure depicts an agent, read it as one participant in the study rather than a
claim about implementation. Its name matters less than the architecture work it
is allowed to perform and the evidence required from that work.

Choose the reading route that matches your purpose:

- **Orientation:** Read the Preface, @sec-moonshot,
  and @sec-what-architect-owns.
- **Research review:** Read the orientation route, then add
  @sec-architecture-20-ontology, @sec-data-representations-world-models,
  @sec-feedback-verification-trust, and the worked study
  in @sec-running-the-loop.
- **Method and environment design:** Read
  @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales through
  @sec-feedback-verification-trust, see the pieces run as one study
  in @sec-running-the-loop, then compare regimes
  in @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack.
- **Full argument:** Read the chapters in order.

For hands-on practice, use the bootstrap path in
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping and the
[companion labs](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/tree/main/labs). The labs
are a separate path for practicing selected parts of executable architecture
work. Their run records make that work inspectable, but the architecture finding
remains the point. The labs complement rather than replace the constructed
lighthouse study in @sec-running-the-loop. Full transfer requires an open-ended
study on a new bounded problem that leaves behind a study brief, method
rationale, tool path, evaluation packet, tested mechanism explanation, and
bounded decision memo.

AI systems do not remove the architect. They raise what the architect must be
good at. The work expands toward choosing abstractions, exposing tacit
constraints, directing tools and experiments, interpreting cross-layer effects,
and deciding what the evidence supports. The opportunity is not to wait for a
system that designs a computer from a single sentence. It is to build the
representations, datasets, environments, methods, experimental practices, and
professional judgment that let architects use AI well. Architecture 2.0 will
matter if it helps them formulate better questions, explore stronger
alternatives, implement checkable artifacts, evaluate them honestly, explain the
mechanisms, and defend decisions that deserve to travel.

Vijay Janapa Reddi
